one_line_takeaway: MC2MARK is a distortion-free multi-bit LLM watermarking scheme (colored multi-channel reweighting + multi-layer composition + evidence-accumulation decoding) that substantially outperforms MPAC and BiMark on long-message detectability and robustness while preserving generation quality -- it is a provenance/watermarking method paper, not an AI-security attack/defense study.
key_finding: For 256-bit messages, MC2MARK achieves at least 91% detection accuracy across the seven datasets/models tested, which the authors report as exceeding the second-best baseline by nearly 30 percentage points. In the main Table 1 (Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct), MC2MARK reaches 100% accuracy on nearly all datasets for n<=32, stays >=94.5% at n=128, and remains 91.3-97.8% at n=256 and 87.2-92.7% at n=512, while BiMark collapses to 36.7-60.6% at n=512 and MPAC(delta=1.5) to roughly 55.6-64.2% at n=512. Under robustness testing (Table 2), MC2MARK retains 100% accuracy at n=16 up to 30% random token replacement (vs. BiMark's 85.8% and MPAC(delta=1.5)'s 87.1% at 30%), and under Dipper paraphrasing at n=16 MC2MARK reaches 72.4% vs. BiMark's 51.6% and MPAC(delta=1.5)'s 66.2%; the robustness advantage narrows but persists at longer message lengths (e.g., at n=512 under Dipper, MC2MARK=54.0% vs. BiMark=44.8%, MPAC(delta=1.5)=52.6%). Distortion-freeness (Table 3) shows MC2MARK's summarization/translation quality metrics (BERTScore 0.3065/0.5480, ROUGE-1 0.3778, perplexity 6.3742, BLEU 20.6516) track closely with the No-Watermark baseline (0.3058/0.5436, 0.3772, 6.4155, 20.2038) and are comparable to or better than BiMark and MPAC on most metrics. The layer-count ablation (Figure 5) shows accuracy saturating around m=20 layers, with diminishing or slightly negative returns beyond that; the mask-bit ablation (Figure 4) shows removing the XOR whitening mask causes a marked accuracy drop at n=64 and n=128 relative to full MC2MARK, and the 'vanilla' single-channel baseline underperforms both MC2MARK variants substantially.
core_contribution: The paper (self-titled MC2MARK in the PDF, though the KG record's title reads 'MCMark') proposes a distortion-free multi-bit watermarking framework for LLM-generated text that scales to long embedded messages. It introduces two novel mechanisms: (1) Multi-Channel Colored Reweighting (MCCR), which partitions the vocabulary into n disjoint subsets, interprets each bit of the message as a 'color' (green=amplify, red=attenuate) for the corresponding subset, and derives a closed-form scaling function (Eq. 14, derived via a constrained optimization over the permutation space of the key) that provably satisfies the distortion-free constraint (Eq. 5c) in expectation; and (2) Multi-Layer Sequential Reweighting (MLSR), which applies MCCR sequentially across m watermark keys/layers per generation step to strengthen the statistical signal. For detection, the paper proposes an evidence-accumulation detector that reconstructs, at each (time step, layer), which vocabulary subset was 'active' and whether that subset's greenness implies each candidate message bit is 0 or 1, then normalizes hit counts by per-bit 'total opportunity' counts (Eq. 19) to correct for bias introduced by the XOR masking of message segments, and decodes each bit by comparing normalized hit rates. A segmentation strategy (splitting the n-bit message into g segments of size n' with a per-step random segment index and XOR mask) is used to keep the per-step reweighting complexity from scaling as O(n choose l) for large n. The paper builds directly on, and generalizes, a prior single-channel scheme the authors call 'MCMARK' (Chen et al., 2025, arXiv:2502.11268) by adding multi-bit (colored) message encoding, multi-layer composition, and the evidence-accumulation decoder -- these three additions are MC2MARK's own contribution, not the cited MCMARK baseline.
threat_model: Not applicable -- this is a watermarking/provenance-tracing method paper, not a security attack/defense paper with an adversarial threat model in the AI-security sense. The paper does consider two non-adversarial-but-adversarial-flavored perturbations as robustness stressors in its evaluation: random token replacement (10/20/30/50% of tokens substituted) and Dipper-model paraphrasing (Krishna et al., 2023) of the watermarked text, both applied post-hoc to test whether the embedded message can still be recovered. It does not model a watermark-removal adversary with white-box access, does not analyze forgeability/spoofing of the watermark, and does not discuss key-security or side-channel leakage of the watermarking key -- these are the typical elements of an AI-security threat model and are absent here.
limitations: The paper's own text and tables show accuracy degrading as message length grows even for MC2MARK itself (from ~99-100% at n=16-64 down to ~87-97% at n=512 depending on dataset/perturbation), so the 'near-perfect for short, +30% for long' framing understates that absolute performance still declines with n; robustness under paraphrasing specifically drops to the 54-72% range at n>=256, which is a much weaker regime than the near-100% detectability numbers reported for clean text. The paper does not evaluate against a watermark-removal-focused adversary beyond random substitution and one paraphrasing tool (Dipper), does not discuss key management/security, does not report computational overhead of the m-layer generation/detection loop beyond a big-O complexity argument for the single-layer channel count, and evaluates only three base models (Qwen2.5-3B, Llama-3.2-3B, Phi-3.5-mini), all in the 3-3.8B parameter range, so generalization to larger or differently-trained models is untested within the paper. The theoretical distortion-free proof (Appendix A) is for the single-layer case; multi-layer distortion-freeness is asserted by citing composition results from other papers (Dathathri et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2025) rather than proven from scratch in this paper.

datasets_used: c4 subset (random subset of C4, Raffel et al., 2020)
quantitative_results: At n=256, MC2MARK achieves >=91% accuracy across datasets/models, exceeding the second-best method by nearly 30 percentage points (paper's headline claim)
Table 1 (Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct), n=512: MC2MARK 84.8-92.7% vs. BiMark 36.7-60.6% vs. MPAC(delta=1.5) 55.6-64.2% across the 7 datasets
Table 2 robustness, n=16, 30% token replacement: MC2MARK 100% vs. BiMark 85.78% vs. MPAC(delta=1.5) 87.13%
Table 2 robustness, n=16, Dipper paraphrasing: MC2MARK 72.40% vs. BiMark 51.56% vs. MPAC(delta=1.5) 66.19%
Table 2 robustness, n=512, Dipper paraphrasing: MC2MARK 54.01% vs. BiMark 44.78% vs. MPAC(delta=1.5) 52.61%
Table 3 distortion-freeness: MC2MARK summarization BERTScore 0.3065 / ROUGE-1 0.3778 / perplexity 6.3742 vs. No-Watermark 0.3058 / 0.3772 / 6.4155
Table 3 distortion-freeness: MC2MARK translation BERTScore 0.5480 / BLEU 20.6516 vs. No-Watermark 0.5436 / 20.2038
Figure 3: at n=128 and 50-token generations, BiMark falls below 30% accuracy while MC2MARK remains above 70%
Figure 5 ablation: accuracy vs. number of layers m saturates around m=20 for longer messages (n up to 512)
Appendix Table 4 (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct), n=512: MC2MARK 94.69-96.06% vs. BiMark 30.11-34.88%
Appendix Table 5 (Phi-3.5-mini-instruct), n=512: MC2MARK 86.20-90.16% vs. BiMark 33.07-37.44%
baselines_compared: MPAC (Yoo et al., 2024), tested at delta=1.0 and delta=1.5
No Watermark (reference baseline for distortion-freeness only)
'Vanilla method' = direct application of prior single-channel MCMARK (Chen et al., 2025) with only one amplified channel (ablation-only baseline)
MC2MARK without mask bit (ablation-only variant of the paper's own method)
